question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a non-network DoS by exploiting the fact that `ConnectToDb` does not implement any backpressure mechanism — when the connection pool is full, new requests block indefinitely in `database/sql`'s internal queue, and a sustained flood of requests fills this queue, consuming memory until the process is OOM-killed?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a non-network DoS by exploiting the fact that `ConnectToDb` does not set `db.SetMaxIdleTime()` (Go 1.15+ feature), allowing idle connections to persist indefinitely, and under a pattern of bursty traffic, the pool accumulates many idle connections that consume database server memory and file descriptors?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: GetDbWithContext()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a non-network DoS by exploiting the fact that `GetDbWithContext()` does not check whether the returned `*gorm.DB` is nil before returning it, and if `d.db` is nil (due to a failed `ConnectToDb`), callers will panic when executing queries, crashing the service?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/db/db.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: ConnectToDb()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a non-network DoS by exploiting the fact that `ConnectToDb` does not implement connection pool pre-warming, causing a thundering herd problem at startup where many concurrent requests all attempt to open new connections simultaneously, overwhelming the database?
